content:
# Exodus Chapter 15 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~41r–42r (PDF pp. 77–79); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 55–56 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_015_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_015_page_002.jpg`, `EXO_015_page_003.jpg`

---

### The Song of the Sea (vv. 1–19)

**1** Then Moses and the Israelites sang this song to YHWH. They sang:

> I will sing to YHWH, for he has triumphed gloriously —
> horse and rider he has hurled into the sea!

**2**

> YHWH is my strength and my song —
> he has become my salvation.
> This is my God, and I will praise him,
> my father's God, and I will exalt him.

**3**

> YHWH is a warrior —
> YHWH is his name!

**4**

> Pharaoh's chariots and his army
> he cast into the sea;
> the best of his officers
> were drowned in the Sea of Reeds.

**5**

> The deep waters covered them —
> they sank to the bottom like a stone.

**6**

> Your right hand, YHWH, is majestic in power;
> your right hand, YHWH, shatters the enemy.

**7**

> In the greatness of your majesty
> you overthrow those who rise against you.
> You unleash your burning anger —
> it consumes them like stubble.

**8**

> At the blast of your nostrils
> the waters piled up;
> the surging waters stood firm like a wall;
> the deep waters congealed in the heart of the sea.

**9**

> The enemy said,
> "I will pursue, I will overtake,
> I will divide the plunder,
> my appetite will gorge on them.
> I will draw my sword —
> my hand will destroy them!"

**10**

> You blew with your wind —
> the sea covered them.
> They sank like lead
> in the mighty waters.

**11**

> Who is like you among the gods, YHWH?
> Who is like you — majestic in holiness,
> awesome in glory, working wonders?

**12**

> You stretched out your right hand —
> the earth swallowed them.

**13**

> In your steadfast love you led
> the people you redeemed;
> in your strength you guided them
> to your holy dwelling place.

**14**

> The peoples heard and trembled;
> anguish gripped those living in Philistia.

**15**

> Then the chiefs of Edom were terrified;
> the leaders of Moab — trembling seized them;
> all who lived in Canaan melted away.

**16**

> Terror and dread fell on them.
> By the power of your arm
> they were still as stone —
> until your people passed by, YHWH,
> until the people you acquired passed by.

**17**

> You will bring them in and plant them
> on the mountain that is your own,
> the place you have made for your dwelling, YHWH,
> the sanctuary, Lord, that your hands established.

**18**

> YHWH will reign
> forever and ever!

---

### The Sea Returns over Egypt (v. 19)

**19** When Pharaoh's horses, chariots, and horsemen went into the sea, YHWH brought the waters of the sea back over them — but the Israelites had walked through the sea on dry ground.

---

### Miriam's Song (vv. 20–21)

**20** Then Miriam the prophetess, Aaron's sister, took a tambourine in her hand, and all the women followed her with tambourines, dancing.

**21** Miriam sang to them:

> Sing to YHWH, for he has triumphed gloriously —
> horse and rider he has hurled into the sea!

---

### Bitter Water at Marah (vv. 22–27)

**22** Then Moses led Israel away from the Sea of Reeds, and they went out into the wilderness of Shur. They traveled three days in the wilderness without finding water.

**23** When they arrived at Marah, they could not drink the water there because it was bitter — that is why the place is called Marah.

**24** The people complained to Moses: "What are we supposed to drink?"

**25** Moses cried out to YHWH, and YHWH showed him a piece of wood. He threw it into the water, and the water became sweet. There YHWH established a statute and a rule for them, and there he tested them.

**26** He said, "If you will truly listen to the voice of YHWH your God, and do what is right in his eyes, and pay attention to his commands, and keep all his statutes, then I will not bring on you any of the diseases I brought on the Egyptians — for I am YHWH, your healer."

**27** Then they came to Elim, where there were twelve springs of water and seventy palm trees, and they camped there by the water.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**v.1 — "I WILL SING TO YHWH" — THE SONG BEGINS (Heb. *az yashir-Mosheh uvnei Yisra'el et-hashirah hazzot laYHWH vayyomru lemor ashirah laYHWH ki-ga'oh ga'ah sus verokehvo ramah vayyam*, אָ֣ז יָשִֽׁיר־מֹשֶׁ֤ה וּבְנֵ֣י יִשְׂרָאֵל֙ אֶת־הַשִּׁירָ֣ה הַזֹּ֔את לַיהוָ֖ה וַיֹּאמְר֣וּ לֵאמֹ֑ר אָשִׁ֤ירָה לַֽיהוָה֙ כִּֽי־גָאֹ֣ה גָּאָ֔ה ס֥וּס וְרֹכְב֖וֹ רָמָ֥ה בַיָּֽם):** The opening word *az* (אז, "then") is a temporal marker that anchors the song in the narrative sequence: this poem erupts from the event described in chapter 14. The verb *yashir* (ישיר, from *shir*, שיר, "to sing") is in the imperfect (yiqtol) form — *az yashir*, "then he sang" — using the imperfect with *az* to narrate a past completed action, a classical Hebrew construction. The rabbis famously noted the imperfect form and read it as "then he will sing," finding in it a hint of future resurrection singing (b. Sanhedrin 91b). While this is midrashic rather than grammatical, it captures something real: this song reverberates beyond its moment.

The phrase *ki-ga'oh ga'ah* (כי גאה גאה, literally "for he has surely risen up / been exalted") uses the infinitive absolute construction (*ga'oh ga'ah*) for emphatic intensification. The root *ga'ah* (גאה) means "to rise up, to be exalted, to surge" — it is also used of rising waters (Ezek. 47:5) and of divine majesty (Isa. 12:5). When applied to YHWH, it carries the double sense of "triumph" and "exaltation" — God has risen up magnificently, and the waters have risen up at his command. The Vaticanus renders this *endoxōs gar dedoxastai* (ἐνδόξως γὰρ δεδόξασται, "for he has been gloriously glorified"), using the *doxa* (glory) root twice.

The refrain — *sus verokehvo ramah vayyam* (סוס ורכבו רמה בים, "horse and its rider he hurled into the sea") — is the song's core image. The verb *ramah* (רמה, "he hurled, he threw, he cast") is a violent verb — not "caused to drown" but "threw." God does not merely allow the Egyptians to perish; he physically throws them. The horse-and-rider unit (*sus verokehvo*) was the ancient world's supreme military technology — the chariot team. YHWH takes the enemy's most fearsome weapon and flings it into the sea like refuse.

**NOTE ON THE LENINGRAD CODEX'S SPECIAL LAYOUT:** The Song of the Sea is written in the Leningrad Codex (and in all Torah scrolls) in a distinctive "brick-like" pattern called *shirah* (שירה, "song") layout, visible on page 2 of the source images. The text alternates between the left and right sides of the column with blank space in between, creating a visual pattern that resembles a brick wall — each "brick" of text sits over a gap below it. This special formatting is prescribed by the Talmud (b. Megillah 16b) as *ariach al gabei leveinah veleveinah al gabei ariach* — "half-brick over whole-brick and whole-brick over half-brick." Only two passages in the Torah receive this treatment: the Song of the Sea (Exod. 15) and the Song of Moses (Deut. 32). The layout is not decorative; it is a visual signal that what follows is not narrative prose but poetry — and not ordinary poetry, but the most ancient and sacred song in the Israelite tradition.

**v.2 — "YHWH IS MY STRENGTH AND MY SONG" — THE *ZIMRAT YAH* CRUX (Heb. *ozzi vezimrat Yah vayehi-li lishu'ah zeh Eli ve'anvehu Elohei avi va'aromemenhu*, עָזִּ֤י וְזִמְרָת֙ יָ֔הּ וַֽיְהִי־לִ֖י לִֽישׁוּעָ֑ה זֶ֤ה אֵלִי֙ וְאַנְוֵ֔הוּ אֱלֹהֵ֥י אָבִ֖י וַאֲרֹמְמֶֽנְהוּ):** This verse contains one of the most debated phrases in Hebrew poetry: *ozzi vezimrat Yah* (עזי וזמרת יה). The word *ozzi* (עזי, "my strength") is clear. But *zimrat* (זמרת) is ambiguous. It could derive from *zamar* (זמר, "to sing, to make music"), yielding "my song" — hence "my strength and my song is Yah." Or it could derive from a different root meaning "protection, defense, might" (cf. Arabic *ḏimr*, "strong"), yielding "my strength and my might is Yah." The construct *zimrat Yah* (זמרת יה) connects the noun directly to the divine name *Yah* (יה), the short form of YHWH. This exact phrase recurs in Isaiah 12:2 and Psalm 118:14, always in contexts of salvation. I have translated "my strength and my song" because the musical connotation resonates with the song context, but the ambiguity is real: YHWH is simultaneously Israel's power and Israel's music.

The phrase *zeh Eli* (זה אלי, "this is my God") is extraordinarily intimate. The demonstrative *zeh* (זה, "this") — a pointing word, a "this one right here" — transforms theology into personal encounter. Not "a god exists" but "this one — this God — is mine." The verb *ve'anvehu* (ואנוהו) is another crux: it may derive from *navah* (נוה, "to adorn, to beautify"), meaning "I will beautify/glorify him," or from *naveh* (נוה, "dwelling"), meaning "I will make a dwelling for him." The rabbis read both: "I will beautify him [in my worship] and I will build him [a sanctuary]" (b. Shabbat 133b). The LXX reads *doxasō auton* (δοξάσω αὐτόν, "I will glorify him").

**v.3 — "YHWH IS A WARRIOR" — THE DIVINE WARRIOR (Heb. *YHWH ish milchamah YHWH shemo*, יְהוָ֖ה אִ֣ישׁ מִלְחָמָ֑ה יְהוָ֖ה שְׁמֽוֹ):** This is one of the most theologically charged declarations in the Hebrew Bible. The phrase *ish milchamah* (איש מלחמה, "man of war / warrior") applies the word *ish* (איש, "man") to YHWH — an anthropomorphism so bold that the LXX avoids it entirely, translating *syntribōn polemous* (συντρίβων πολέμους, "one who shatters wars") — turning the divine warrior into a peacemaker, softening the image considerably. The Vaticanus preserves this LXX reading. The Hebrew text, however, does not flinch: YHWH is a warrior. The word *milchamah* (מלחמה, from *lacham*, לחם, "to fight") — the same root as *lechem* (לחם, "bread"), since warfare and sustenance share a semantic field in the ancient world — defines YHWH's character in this moment. He does not merely permit victory; he fights.

The second clause — *YHWH shemo* (יהוה שמו, "YHWH is his name") — provides the warrior's identity. The *shem* (שם, "name") of God is his revealed character. The warrior is not anonymous; he has told Israel who he is (3:14-15). The name YHWH, revealed at the burning bush, is now confirmed at the sea. What the bush promised, the sea delivers.

**v.4 — PHARAOH'S CHARIOTS CAST INTO THE SEA (Heb. *markevot Par'oh vecheilo yarah vayyam umivchar shalishav tubba'u veYam-Suf*, מַרְכְּבֹ֥ת פַּרְעֹ֛ה וְחֵיל֖וֹ יָרָ֣ה בַיָּ֑ם וּמִבְחַ֥ר שָֽׁלִשָׁ֖יו טֻבְּע֥וּ בְיַם־סֽוּף):** The verb *yarah* (ירה, "he cast, he shot, he hurled") is the verb used for shooting arrows (1 Sam. 20:36) and casting lots (Josh. 18:6). YHWH is the archer; the chariots are his projectiles. The verb *tubba'u* (טבעו, from *tava'*, טבע, "to sink, to be submerged") describes the drowning — they "were sunk" in the passive, as objects acted upon. The word *mivchar* (מבחר, "the choicest, the elite, the best of") — from *bachar* (בחר, "to choose") — specifies that it was not the expendable soldiers who drowned but the elite officers, the *shalishim* (שלשים), Pharaoh's best and bravest.

**v.5 — "THEY SANK LIKE A STONE" (Heb. *tehomot yekhaseyumu yardu vimtsolot kemo-aven*, תְּהֹמֹ֖ת יְכַסְיֻ֑מוּ יָרְד֥וּ בִמְצוֹלֹ֖ת כְּמוֹ־אָֽבֶן):** The word *tehomot* (תהמות, plural of *tehom*, תהום, "the deep, the abyss") is a cosmological term reaching back to Genesis 1:2, where *tehom* is the primordial deep over which God's spirit hovers. The *tehom* is not merely "deep water" — it is the chaos-deep, the pre-creation abyss. The Egyptians are swallowed not just by the sea but by the cosmic forces that existed before the ordered world. The word *metsolot* (מצולות, "depths, ocean-floor") intensifies the descent — they went all the way down. The simile *kemo-aven* (כמו אבן, "like a stone") is the first of three sinking similes in the poem: like a stone (v.5), like lead (v.10), and like stubble consumed (v.7). Each captures a different quality: the stone's weight, the lead's heaviness, the stubble's flammability.

**v.6 — "YOUR RIGHT HAND, YHWH" — THE THEOLOGY OF THE RIGHT HAND (Heb. *yeminekha YHWH ne'dari bakkoach yeminekha YHWH tir'ats oyev*, יְמִֽינְךָ֣ יְהוָ֔ה נֶאְדָּרִ֖י בַּכֹּ֑חַ יְמִֽינְךָ֥ יְהוָ֖ה תִּרְעַ֥ץ אוֹיֵֽב):** The repetition of *yeminekha YHWH* (ימינך יהוה, "your right hand, YHWH") at the beginning of each line is an example of *anaphora* — deliberate repetition for rhetorical force. The right hand (*yamin*, ימין) is the hand of power, authority, and favor in the ancient Near East. The verb *ne'dari* (נאדרי, from *adar*, אדר, "to be majestic, to be magnificent") — a rare word appearing also in v.11 — describes the hand's quality: it is "majestic" or "glorious" in power. The verb *tir'ats* (תרעץ, from *ra'ats*, רעץ, "to shatter, to crush") is violent — the right hand does not merely defeat but pulverizes. The *oyev* (אויב, "enemy") is singular and collective: Egypt, but also every enemy of God's people.

**v.7 — "YOU OVERTHROW THOSE WHO RISE AGAINST YOU" (Heb. *uverov ge'onekha taharos qamekha teshalach charonekha yokhlemo kaqqash*, וּבְרֹ֥ב גְּאוֹנְךָ֖ תַּהֲרֹ֣ס קָמֶ֑יךָ תְּשַׁלַּח֙ חֲרֹ֣נְךָ֔ יֹאכְלֵ֖מוֹ כַּקַּֽשׁ):** The word *ge'on* (גאון, "majesty, pride, rising") shares the root *ga'ah* with v.1's *ga'oh ga'ah* — the same "rising up" that described YHWH's triumph now describes his overwhelming majesty. The verb *taharos* (תהרס, from *haras*, הרס, "to tear down, to demolish, to overthrow") is an architectural verb — used for demolishing walls and buildings (Judg. 6:25). Those who "rise against" God (*qamekha*, from *qum*, קום, "to rise") are themselves torn down. The irony is structural: those who rise are demolished.

The word *charon* (חרון, "burning anger, fierce wrath") — always used of divine anger in the Hebrew Bible, never of human anger — is YHWH's signature fury. Combined with *yokhlemo* (יאכלמו, from *akhal*, אכל, "to eat, to consume, to devour") and *kaqqash* (כקש, "like stubble/chaff"), the image is of enemies consumed by fire: YHWH's wrath eats them like flames eat dry straw. The speed and totality are the point — stubble does not smolder; it flashes and is gone.

**v.8 — "AT THE BLAST OF YOUR NOSTRILS" — COSMIC IMAGERY (Heb. *uveruach appekha ne'ermu mayim nitssevu khemo-ned nozelim qafe'u tehomot belev-yam*, וּבְר֤וּחַ אַפֶּ֨יךָ֙ נֶעֶרְמ֣וּ מַ֔יִם נִצְּב֥וּ כְמוֹ־נֵ֖ד נֹזְלִ֑ים קָֽפְא֥וּ תְהֹמֹ֖ת בְּלֶב־יָֽם):** This verse is the cosmic center of the poem. The phrase *ruach appekha* (רוח אפיך, "wind/breath of your nostrils") transforms the "east wind" of 14:21 into divine breath — God's nostril-blast. The word *ruach* (רוח, "wind/breath/spirit") and *af* (אף, "nostril/face/anger") combine: the same wind that physically drove the sea back is theologically the breathing of an angry God. This is not meteorology; it is theology in the language of the body.

The verb *ne'ermu* (נערמו, from *aram*, ערם, "to be heaped up, to be piled") describes the waters being "heaped up" or "piled." The word *ned* (נד, "heap, wall, dam") describes the piled waters as a wall or dam — the same image as *chomah* ("wall") in 14:22 but using a different word. The verb *qafe'u* (קפאו, from *qafa'*, קפא, "to congeal, to freeze, to thicken") is extraordinary: the deep waters *congealed* — became solid, like ice or jelly. Liquid became solid. The *tehomot* (תהומות, "the deeps") — the primordial chaos-waters — froze in the *lev-yam* (לב ים, "the heart of the sea"). The sea has a heart, and its heart stopped. The cosmic imagery fuses creation theology (God commands the waters) with warrior theology (God freezes the enemy's terrain). The Vaticanus reads *epagē ta kymata en mesō tēs thalassēs* — "the waves congealed in the midst of the sea."

**v.9 — THE ENEMY'S BOAST — SIX VERBS OF GREED (Heb. *amar oyev erdof assig achallq shalal timla'emo nafshi ariq charbi torishemo yadi*, אָמַ֥ר אוֹיֵ֛ב אֶרְדֹּ֥ף אַשִּׂ֖יג אֲחַלֵּ֣ק שָׁלָ֑ל תִּמְלָאֵ֣מוֹ נַפְשִׁ֔י אָרִ֣יק חַרְבִּ֔י תּוֹרִישֵׁ֖מוֹ יָדִֽי):** This verse is a masterpiece of poetic compression. Six verbs in rapid-fire staccato capture the enemy's breathless greed: *erdof* (ארדף, "I will pursue"), *assig* (אשיג, "I will overtake"), *achallq* (אחלק, "I will divide"), *timla'emo nafshi* (תמלאמו נפשי, "my throat/appetite will be filled with them"), *ariq charbi* (אריק חרבי, "I will draw/empty my sword"), *torishemo yadi* (תורישמו ידי, "my hand will dispossess/destroy them"). No conjunctions, no pauses — just verb after verb, a breathless panting of predatory desire. The word *nafshi* (נפשי, "my soul/appetite/throat") here means "appetite" — the enemy's hunger for plunder is visceral, physical, located in the gut. The word *shalal* (שלל, "plunder, spoil") is what the enemy desires; the irony is that the sea will spoil the spoiler. The verb *ariq* (אריק, from *ruq*, ריק, "to empty, to pour out") used of the sword means to "empty" it from the scabbard — draw it, make it ready — but the root also means "to make empty," hinting that the sword will accomplish nothing. The six verbs are answered by one divine breath in v.10.

**v.10 — "YOU BLEW — THE SEA COVERED THEM" (Heb. *nashafta veruachakha kissamo yam tsalalu ka'oferet bemayim addirim*, נָשַׁ֥פְתָּ בְרוּחֲךָ֖ כִּסָּ֣מוֹ יָ֑ם צָֽלְל֛וּ כַּעוֹפֶ֖רֶת בְּמַ֥יִם אַדִּירִֽם):** Against the enemy's six verbs, God's response is one verb: *nashafta* (נשפת, from *nashaf*, נשף, "to blow"). One divine breath undoes six acts of human aggression. The verb *kissamo* (כסמו, from *kasah*, כסה, "to cover") — the sea "covered them" — is the same verb used in v.5 (*yekhaseyumu*). The simile *ka'oferet* (כעופרת, "like lead") is the second sinking image — heavier than stone (v.5), lead sinks faster and more decisively. The *mayim addirim* (מים אדירים, "mighty/majestic waters") uses *addir* (אדיר, "mighty, majestic, noble"), a word elsewhere used of God himself (Ps. 93:4) and of human rulers. The waters are "noble" — they serve the divine warrior with the dignity of cosmic soldiers.

**v.11 — "WHO IS LIKE YOU AMONG THE GODS?" — THE THEOLOGICAL SUMMIT (Heb. *mi-khamokha ba'elim YHWH mi kamokha ne'dar baqqodesh nora tehillot oseh fele*, מִֽי־כָמֹ֤כָה בָּֽאֵלִם֙ יְהוָ֔ה מִ֥י כָּמֹ֖כָה נֶאְדָּ֣ר בַּקֹּ֑דֶשׁ נוֹרָ֥א תְהִלֹּ֖ת עֹ֥שֵׂה פֶֽלֶא):** This is the theological climax of the entire poem and one of the most important verses in the Hebrew Bible for understanding Israelite monotheism. The question *mi-khamokha ba'elim* (מי כמכה באלם, "who is like you among the gods?") uses *elim* (אלם), the plural of *el* (אל, "god, divine being"). The question presupposes the existence of other *elim* — other divine beings — and asserts YHWH's incomparability among them. This is not philosophical monotheism ("no other gods exist") but what scholars call *henotheism* or *monolatry* — the acknowledgment that other divine beings may exist but that YHWH is categorically supreme, incomparable, in a class by himself. The question is rhetorical: no one is like YHWH. The other *elim* are real enough to serve as a comparison class, but they cannot compete.

The word *ne'dar* (נאדר, "majestic, glorious") from v.6 returns here — *ne'dar baqqodesh* (נאדר בקדש, "majestic in holiness"). The *qodesh* (קדש, "holiness, sacredness, separateness") is YHWH's defining attribute — his radical otherness, his dangerous separateness from all that is profane or common. The phrase *nora tehillot* (נורא תהלות, "awesome/fearful of praises" or "to be praised with awe") is unusual: the combination of *nora* (נורא, "fearful, terrible, awesome") with *tehillot* (תהלות, "praises, songs of praise") creates a paradox — praise that trembles, worship mixed with terror. And *oseh fele* (עשה פלא, "doing wonder") — the singular *fele* (פלא, "wonder, marvel") rather than the expected plural *fela'ot* — focuses on the singular, unrepeatable wonder of the sea event. The LXX reads *thaumasta en hagiois* (θαυμαστὰ ἐν ἁγίοις, "wonderful among holy ones"), shifting from abstract holiness to holy beings — angels or saints.

**v.12 — "THE EARTH SWALLOWED THEM" (Heb. *natita yeminekha tivla'emo arets*, נָטִ֨יתָ֙ יְמִ֣ינְךָ֔ תִּבְלָעֵ֖מוֹ אָֽרֶץ):** The verb *tivla'emo* (תבלעמו, from *bala'*, בלע, "to swallow, to engulf") with *arets* (ארץ, "earth/land") as subject creates a problem: the Egyptians drowned in the *sea*, not the earth. Some scholars read *arets* as "the underworld" (Sheol, the land of the dead); others see a merism in which both sea and earth participate in the destruction. The verb *bala'* (בלע, "to swallow") will become the defining verb of Korah's rebellion (Num. 16:32), where the earth literally opens and swallows the rebels. The right hand (*yamin*) returns from v.6 — YHWH stretched it out, and the earth obeyed.

**v.13 — "IN YOUR STEADFAST LOVE" — *CHESED* (Heb. *nachita vechasdekha am-zu ga'alta nehalta ve'ozzekha el-neveh qodshekha*, נָחִ֥יתָ בְחַסְדְּךָ֖ עַם־ז֣וּ גָּאָ֑לְתָּ נֵהַ֥לְתָּ בְעָזְּךָ֖ אֶל־נְוֵ֥ה קָדְשֶֽׁךָ):** The word *chesed* (חסד, "steadfast love, loyal love, covenant faithfulness, mercy, lovingkindness") is one of the most theologically loaded words in the Hebrew Bible. It appears here for the first time in the poetic tradition of Israel. *Chesed* is not merely kindness — it is covenantal loyalty, the commitment of a stronger party to a weaker party, the love that endures when the beloved is unworthy. The verb *nachita* (נחית, from *nachah*, נחה, "to lead, to guide") describes shepherding — God leads his people like a shepherd leads a flock.

The verb *ga'alta* (גאלת, from *ga'al*, גאל, "to redeem, to act as kinsman-redeemer") introduces the *go'el* (גואל, "redeemer") concept — YHWH is Israel's next-of-kin who buys them back from slavery. The *go'el* in Israelite law was the family member responsible for redeeming a relative from debt-slavery or avenging their blood. YHWH assumes this family role toward Israel. The phrase *neveh qodshekha* (נוה קדשך, "the dwelling/pasture of your holiness") — *neveh* (נוה, "pasture, abode, dwelling") — could refer to the promised land, to Mount Sinai, or proleptically to the Jerusalem temple. The ambiguity is productive: all three are "holy dwelling places" where YHWH will bring his people.

**vv.14-16 — THE NATIONS TREMBLE (Heb. *shame'u ammim yirgazun chil achaz yoshevei Pelashet; az nivhalu allufei Edom eilei Mo'av yochaezemo ra'ad namogu kol yoshevei Khena'an; tippol aleihem eimatah vafachad bigdol zero'akha yiddemu ka'aven ad-ya'avor ammekha YHWH ad-ya'avor am-zu qanita*, שָֽׁמְע֥וּ עַמִּ֖ים יִרְגָּז֑וּן חִ֣יל אָחַ֔ז יֹשְׁבֵ֖י פְּלָֽשֶׁת אָ֤ז נִבְהֲלוּ֙ אַלּוּפֵ֣י אֱד֔וֹם אֵילֵ֣י מוֹאָ֔ב יֹאחֲזֵ֖מוֹ רָ֑עַד נָמֹ֕גוּ כֹּ֖ל יֹשְׁבֵ֥י כְנָֽעַן תִּפֹּ֨ל עֲלֵיהֶ֤ם אֵימָ֨תָה֙ וָפַ֔חַד בִּגְדֹ֥ל זְרוֹעֲךָ֖ יִדְּמ֣וּ כָאָ֑בֶן עַד־יַעֲבֹ֤ר עַמְּךָ֙ יְהוָ֔ה עַֽד־יַעֲבֹ֖ר עַם־ז֥וּ קָנִֽיתָ):** The song shifts from past deliverance to its future effects on the nations. The geographical sweep — Philistia, Edom, Moab, Canaan — traces Israel's coming route from Egypt to the promised land. The verb *yirgazun* (ירגזון, from *ragaz*, רגז, "to tremble, to quake") describes seismic terror. The word *chil* (חיל, "writhing, labor pain, anguish") — the same word as *chayil* ("army, strength") but from a different root meaning "to writhe" — describes the pain of childbirth. The nations writhe like women in labor at the news of YHWH's victory.

The verb *namogu* (נמגו, from *mug*, מוג, "to melt, to dissolve") — the Canaanites "melted" — anticipates Rahab's confession in Joshua 2:9-11, where she reports that the inhabitants of Jericho have "melted" at the news of the sea crossing. The verb *yiddemu* (ידמו, from *damam*, דמם, "to be silent, to be still, to be dumb") means the nations become "still as stone" — paralyzed, unable to move or speak. The word *qanita* (קנית, from *qanah*, קנה, "to acquire, to purchase, to create") describes Israel as God's "acquired" or "purchased" people — *am-zu qanita* (עם זו קנית, "the people whom you have acquired"). The verb *qanah* can mean both "to buy" and "to create" (as in Gen. 14:19, *qoneh shamayim va'arets*, "creator of heaven and earth"), suggesting that Israel's redemption is simultaneously a purchase and a new creation.

**v.17 — "THE MOUNTAIN THAT IS YOUR OWN" — TEMPLE THEOLOGY (Heb. *tevi'emo vetita'emo behar nachalatekha makhon leshivtekha pa'alta YHWH miqqedash Adonai konenu yadekha*, תְּבִאֵ֗מוֹ וְתִטָּעֵ֨מוֹ֙ בְּהַ֣ר נַחֲלָתְךָ֔ מָכ֧וֹן לְשִׁבְתְּךָ֛ פָּעַ֖לְתָּ יְהוָ֑ה מִקְּדָ֕שׁ אֲדֹנָ֖י כּוֹנְנ֥וּ יָדֶֽיךָ):** This verse is the theological destination of the entire song. The verbs *tevi'emo* (תבאמו, "you will bring them in") and *vetita'emo* (ותטעמו, "and you will plant them") use agricultural imagery — Israel will be "planted" like a tree on God's mountain. The *har nachalatekha* (הר נחלתך, "the mountain of your inheritance/possession") is ambiguous: it could refer to Sinai, to Mount Zion, or to the entire promised land conceived as YHWH's mountain. The word *makhon* (מכון, "established place, foundation, fixed place") — from *kun* (כון, "to be firm, to be established") — describes a place YHWH has prepared, a pre-existing divine dwelling. The word *miqqedash* (מקדש, "sanctuary, holy place") explicitly introduces temple theology: YHWH has (or will have) a physical dwelling among his people. The phrase *konenu yadekha* (כוננו ידיך, "your hands have established") uses the dual *yadekha* (ידיך, "your two hands") rather than the singular — God builds his sanctuary with both hands, emphasizing the personal, artisanal quality of divine construction.

Many scholars consider this verse (and vv.13-17 generally) to be later additions to an originally shorter poem, reflecting the theology of the Jerusalem temple. Others argue the mountain-of-God concept is pre-monarchic and need not presuppose Solomon's temple. The debate is unresolved, but the text as it stands connects the sea event to the mountain event — the deliverance at the water leads to the dwelling on the mountain.

**v.18 — "YHWH WILL REIGN FOREVER AND EVER" (Heb. *YHWH yimlokh le'olam va'ed*, יְהוָ֥ה ׀ יִמְלֹ֖ךְ לְעֹלָ֥ם וָעֶֽד):** The song's climactic declaration. The verb *yimlokh* (ימלך, from *malakh*, מלך, "to reign, to be king") — the imperfect expressing eternal, ongoing reality — declares YHWH's eternal kingship. The phrase *le'olam va'ed* (לעלם ועד, "forever and ever," literally "to eternity and perpetuity") doubles the temporal markers for emphasis. This is the seed of the *YHWH malakh* (יהוה מלך, "YHWH reigns!") psalms (Pss. 93, 97, 99) and of all subsequent Israelite kingship theology. The Song of the Sea, in its final word, declares that the divine warrior who won the battle is the divine king who reigns eternally. Victory is not temporary; it establishes permanent sovereignty.

**v.19 — THE PROSE SUMMARY (Heb. *ki va sus Par'oh berikhevu uveparashav bayyam vayyashev YHWH aleihem et-mei hayyam uvnei Yisra'el halekhu vayyabbashah betokh hayyam*, כִּ֣י בָא֩ ס֨וּס פַּרְעֹ֜ה בְּרִכְבּ֤וֹ וּבְפָרָשָׁיו֙ בַּיָּ֔ם וַיָּ֧שֶׁב יְהוָ֛ה עֲלֵהֶ֖ם אֶת־מֵ֣י הַיָּ֑ם וּבְנֵ֧י יִשְׂרָאֵ֛ל הָלְכ֥וּ בַיַּבָּשָׁ֖ה בְּת֥וֹךְ הַיָּֽם):** This verse functions as a prose colophon to the poetic song, recapitulating the core narrative: Pharaoh's horses entered the sea; YHWH brought the waters back; Israel walked through on dry ground. The shift from poetry back to prose marks the song's formal conclusion and bridges back to the narrative framework.

**v.20 — MIRIAM THE PROPHETESS (Heb. *vattiqqach Miryam hanevi'ah achot Aharon et-hattof beyadah vattetsena khol-hannashim achareiha betuppim uvimcholot*, וַתִּקַּח֩ מִרְיָ֨ם הַנְּבִיאָ֜ה אֲח֧וֹת אַהֲרֹ֛ן אֶת־הַתֹּ֖ף בְּיָדָ֑הּ וַתֵּצֶ֤אןָ כָֽל־הַנָּשִׁים֙ אַחֲרֶ֔יהָ בְּתֻפִּ֖ים וּבִמְחֹלֹֽת):** Miriam is called *hanevi'ah* (הנביאה, "the prophetess") — this is the first time in the Hebrew Bible that any woman receives this title. The word *navi'* (נביא, "prophet") — and its feminine form *nevi'ah* (נביאה) — designates one who speaks for God. Miriam's prophetic identity is stated without explanation or qualification; it is presented as already known, already established. She is identified as *achot Aharon* (אחות אהרן, "sister of Aaron") rather than "sister of Moses" — a detail that has generated much rabbinic and scholarly discussion. Some suggest this reflects an older tradition in which Miriam's primary association was with Aaron rather than Moses; others note that Moses is the speaker/singer of the preceding song, so identifying Miriam in relation to him would be redundant.

The *tof* (תף, "tambourine, hand-drum") was a small frame drum played by striking with the palm — a standard instrument for women's victory celebrations in ancient Israel (cf. Judg. 11:34; 1 Sam. 18:6). The *mecholot* (מחלות, "dances, round-dances") — from *chul/chol* (חול, "to whirl, to dance") — describe ecstatic circle-dancing. The scene is one of collective female liturgical expression: Miriam leads, the women follow, with drums and dancing. This is not background activity; it is the climactic worship response to the sea event.

**v.21 — MIRIAM'S REFRAIN (Heb. *vatta'an lahem Miryam shiru laYHWH ki-ga'oh ga'ah sus verokehvo ramah vayyam*, וַתַּ֥עַן לָהֶ֖ם מִרְיָ֑ם שִׁ֤ירוּ לַֽיהוָה֙ כִּֽי־גָאֹ֣ה גָּאָ֔ה ס֥וּס וְרֹכְב֖וֹ רָמָ֥ה בַיָּֽם):** The verb *vatta'an* (ותען, from *anah*, ענה, "to answer, to respond, to sing responsively") indicates antiphonal singing — Miriam "responded" or "sang back." This implies a call-and-response structure: Moses (or a lead singer) sings, and Miriam leads the women in the refrain. The imperative *shiru* (שירו, "sing!" — plural) replaces Moses' first-person *ashirah* ("I will sing") with a communal command. Many scholars believe Miriam's two-line song is actually the oldest form of the tradition — the original victory cry that was later expanded into the full Song of the Sea. If so, the kernel of the entire poem is a woman's voice.

**vv.22-23 — MARAH: BITTER WATER (Heb. *vayyassa Mosheh et-Yisra'el miYam-Suf vayyets'u el-midbar-Shur vayyelekkhu sheloshet-yamim bammidbar velo-mats'u mayim; vayyavo'u Maratah velo yakhlu lishtot mayim miMarah ki marim hem al-ken qara-shemah Marah*, וַיַּסַּ֨ע מֹשֶׁ֤ה אֶת־יִשְׂרָאֵל֙ מִיַּם־ס֔וּף וַיֵּצְא֖וּ אֶל־מִדְבַּר־שׁ֑וּר וַיֵּלְכ֧וּ שְׁלֹֽשֶׁת־יָמִ֛ים בַּמִּדְבָּ֖ר וְלֹא־מָ֥צְאוּ מָֽיִם וַיָּבֹ֣אוּ מָרָ֔תָה וְלֹ֣א יָֽכְל֗וּ לִשְׁתֹּ֥ת מַ֨יִם֙ מִמָּרָ֔ה כִּ֥י מָרִ֖ים הֵ֑ם עַל־כֵּ֥ן קָרָֽא־שְׁמָ֖הּ מָרָֽה):** The narrative shift from triumph to crisis is abrupt and deliberate. Three days (*sheloshet yamim*) without water in the wilderness of Shur — and when water is found, it is *marim* (מרים, "bitter"). The place name *Marah* (מרה) comes from *mar* (מר, "bitter"), creating an etymology: "That is why it was called Bitter." The bitter water is a test — can Israel trust the God who parted the sea to provide water in the desert? The answer, immediately, is no.

**v.24 — THE PEOPLE COMPLAINED (Heb. *vayyillonu ha'am al-Mosheh lemor mah-nishteh*, וַיִּלֹּ֧נוּ הָעָ֛ם עַל־מֹשֶׁ֖ה לֵאמֹ֑ר מַה־נִּשְׁתֶּֽה):** The verb *vayyillonu* (וילנו, from *lun*, לון, "to murmur, to complain, to grumble") introduces the wilderness complaint motif that will dominate the next forty years. This is the first use of this verb in the wilderness narrative — the inaugural grumble. The question *mah-nishteh* (מה נשתה, "what shall we drink?") is reasonable on its face but theologically devastating: they have just seen the sea split and now cannot trust God for drinking water. The faith born at the seashore (14:31) has lasted three days.

**v.25 — THE WOOD THAT SWEETENS (Heb. *vayyits'aq el-YHWH vayyorehu YHWH ets vayyashhlekh el-hammayim vayyimtequ hammayim sham sam lo choq umishpat vesham nissahu*, וַיִּצְעַ֣ק אֶל־יְהוָ֗ה וַיּוֹרֵ֤הוּ יְהוָה֙ עֵ֔ץ וַיַּשְׁלֵךְ֙ אֶל־הַמַּ֔יִם וַֽיִּמְתְּק֖וּ הַמָּ֑יִם שָׁ֣ם שָׂ֥ם ל֛וֹ חֹ֥ק וּמִשְׁפָּ֖ט וְשָׁ֥ם נִסָּֽהוּ):** The verb *vayyorehu* (ויורהו, from *yarah*, ירה, "to teach, to instruct, to show") — YHWH "taught" or "showed" Moses a tree/piece of wood. The root *yarah* is the same root that gives us *Torah* (תורה, "instruction, teaching, law"). The word *ets* (עץ, "tree, wood") is the remedy — a specific tree or branch that, when thrown into bitter water, makes it sweet. The verb *vayyimtequ* (וימתקו, from *mataq*, מתק, "to become sweet") is the transformation: bitter becomes sweet. The pairing of bitterness and sweetness, of poison water and healing wood, becomes a prophetic pattern: the curse is reversed by an unlikely instrument.

The phrase *sham sam lo choq umishpat* (שם שם לו חק ומשפט, "there he established for him a statute and an ordinance") is enigmatic. What statute? What ordinance? The text does not specify. Some scholars connect it to the conditional promise in v.26; others see it as a fragment of a lost tradition. The verb *nissahu* (נסהו, from *nasah*, נסה, "to test, to try, to prove") — "there he tested him/them" — makes clear that the bitter water was not an accident but a divine test, the first of many in the wilderness.

**v.26 — "I AM YHWH, YOUR HEALER" (Heb. *vayyomer im-shamoa' tishma' leqol YHWH Elohekha vehayashar be'einav ta'aseh veha'azanta lemitsvotav veshamarta kol-chuqqav kol-hammachalah asher-samti veMitsrayim lo-asim alekha ki ani YHWH rofe'ekha*, וַיֹּ֩אמֶר֩ אִם־שָׁמ֨וֹעַ תִּשְׁמַ֜ע לְק֣וֹל ׀ יְהוָ֣ה אֱלֹהֶ֗יךָ וְהַיָּשָׁ֤ר בְּעֵינָיו֙ תַּעֲשֶׂ֔ה וְהַאֲזַנְתָּ֖ לְמִצְוֺתָ֑יו וְשָׁמַרְתָּ֙ כָּל־חֻקָּ֔יו כָּל־הַמַּחֲלָ֞ה אֲשֶׁר־שַׂ֤מְתִּי בְמִצְרַ֨יִם֙ לֹא־אָשִׂ֣ים עָלֶ֔יךָ כִּ֛י אֲנִ֥י יְהוָ֖ה רֹפְאֶֽךָ):** The conditional structure — *im-shamoa' tishma'* (אם שמוע תשמע, "if you will truly listen," using the infinitive absolute for emphasis) — establishes the covenant pattern: obedience yields protection. The diseases (*machalah*, מחלה, from *chalah*, חלה, "to be sick, to be weak") placed on Egypt are the plagues, now reframed as diseases. The promise is not merely "I will not afflict you" but something far more profound: *ki ani YHWH rofe'ekha* (כי אני יהוה רפאך, "for I am YHWH, your healer").

The participle *rofe'ekha* (רפאך, from *rafa'*, רפא, "to heal, to cure, to restore") reveals a new divine name/title: YHWH *Rofe'* — YHWH the Healer. The God who inflicted the plagues on Egypt is the same God who heals Israel. The word *rafa'* means not just medical healing but restoration to wholeness — the same verb is used for healing waters (2 Kings 2:21-22), healing land (2 Chron. 7:14), and healing the brokenhearted (Ps. 147:3). The bitter water of Marah becomes the setting for this revelation: the God who sweetened the poison water is the God who heals all disease. The Marah episode thus functions as a theological bridge between the sea deliverance and the Sinai covenant — God the Warrior (v.3) is also God the Healer (v.26).

**v.27 — ELIM: TWELVE SPRINGS AND SEVENTY PALMS (Heb. *vayyavo'u Elimah vesham shteim esreh einot mayim veshiv'im temarim vayyachanu-sham al-hammayim*, וַיָּבֹ֣אוּ אֵילִ֔מָה וְשָׁ֗ם שְׁתֵּ֥ים עֶשְׂרֵ֛ה עֵינֹ֥ת מַ֖יִם וְשִׁבְעִ֣ים תְּמָרִ֑ים וַיַּחֲנוּ־שָׁ֖ם עַל־הַמָּֽיִם):** After the bitter water of Marah, the sweet abundance of Elim. The numbers are symbolic: *shteim esreh* (שתים עשרה, "twelve") springs — one for each tribe of Israel — and *shiv'im* (שבעים, "seventy") palm trees — the number of the nations in Genesis 10, or of Jacob's family who descended to Egypt (Gen. 46:27). The name *Elim* (אילם) may derive from *elim* (אילים, "strong ones, terebinths") or *el* (אל, "god/mighty one"), suggesting a place of divine strength. After the test of Marah, Elim is the oasis of grace — a place of abundant water and shade, rest after trial, provision after complaint. The chapter ends not with a sermon but with a campsite by water under palm trees, the simplest and most profound image of divine care.

---

**The theology of Exodus 15 — triumph, terror, and the turn to wilderness:**

This chapter contains two of the most important theological moments in the Hebrew Bible, set side by side in deliberate contrast: the Song of the Sea (vv.1-21), the most triumphant passage in all of Scripture, and the Marah episode (vv.22-27), the first failure of wilderness faith. Together they establish the pattern that will define the rest of the Pentateuch: spectacular divine action followed immediately by human doubt.

**1. The Song as theology.** The Song of the Sea is not a report about God; it is a poem addressed to God. Its theology is doxological — it arises from worship, not from reflection. The key theological moves are: (a) YHWH is a warrior who personally fights for his people (v.3); (b) YHWH is incomparable among the gods (v.11); (c) YHWH's victory at the sea inaugurates an eternal reign (v.18); (d) the sea event has cosmic implications — the nations tremble, the promised land awaits, the temple is foreshadowed (vv.13-17). The poem moves from past event (the drowning) to present praise (the song) to future consummation (the mountain sanctuary). It is simultaneously a war cry, a creation hymn, and a prophecy.

**2. The *elim* question.** Verse 11's "Who is like you among the gods?" is the hinge of Israelite theology. The question does not deny the existence of other divine beings; it asserts YHWH's radical incomparability. This is the seedbed from which full monotheism will eventually grow — but at this stage, the claim is not "no other gods exist" but "no other god can do what YHWH does." The incomparability is demonstrated by action (the sea), not by metaphysical argument. Israel's monotheism is historical before it is philosophical.

**3. Miriam's voice.** The identification of Miriam as *nevi'ah* (prophetess) in v.20, and the strong scholarly case that her two-line refrain (v.21) is the oldest form of the victory tradition, raises a profound point: the foundational poem of Israel's faith may have originated as a woman's song. The full Song of the Sea attributed to Moses may be an expansion of Miriam's original outcry. If so, the longest and most theologically sophisticated poem in the Torah grows from a root that is female, ecstatic, and musical — a woman with a drum, dancing at the sea's edge, shouting the refrain that became a nation's creed.

**4. Three days from triumph to complaint.** The Marah episode (vv.22-27) is devastating in its narrative placement. The people who sang "YHWH is my strength and my song" (v.2) are, three days later, grumbling "What are we supposed to drink?" (v.24). The faith of 14:31 — born from seeing the great hand of YHWH — cannot survive three days without water. This is not a failure of memory; it is a revelation of what faith costs when the miracle fades and the desert begins. The wilderness will test whether Israel's *emunah* is a response to spectacle or a settled trust in character. The answer, repeatedly, will be painful.

**5. YHWH Rofe' — the Healer.** The divine self-identification *ani YHWH rofe'ekha* ("I am YHWH your healer") in v.26 introduces a dimension of God's character that the sea event alone could not reveal. The warrior who destroys is also the healer who restores. The God who inflicted disease on Egypt promises health to Israel — conditionally, dependent on obedience. The Marah wood that sweetens bitter water is the first sign that YHWH's power is not only destructive but restorative. The warrior heals. The destroyer mends. The same hand that hurled the horse and rider into the sea can also cure what is broken.

— *PNEUMA*
